finding: §3.1 says a present workflow field in a pre-existing or imported task file "is always treated as an override regardless of value", while §3.5 says an out-of-range value (review_clean_rounds: 0) "is a validation error ... never a silent fallback" but pins enforcement to "the point of write (§6.1)", and §6.1 defines range validation only for tp set --workflow. The spec never states that tp import (which §3.2 lists as a WriteTaskFile writer) or authored-file loading enforces the §6.1 ranges, so an imported/hand-authored out-of-range workflow value is honored "regardless of value" per §3.1 — exactly the silent fallback §3.5 says can never occur.
suggestion: State explicitly whether tp import / task-file load validates workflow-field ranges. Either extend the §6.1 range check to every write/load path (so §3.5's "never a silent fallback" holds for imported/authored files too), or narrow §3.5 to acknowledge that legacy/imported out-of-range values are accepted as-is per §3.1's "regardless of value".

finding: §3.2 and §10.2 list `tp import` among the round-trip-preserving commands, but import is a file→file operation (it reads a source and overwrites/creates the task file, `--force` discarding any prior target), not an in-place rewrite. "Leaves the set of present workflow fields unchanged" is ambiguous for import: unchanged relative to the imported SOURCE (correct) versus the overwritten TARGET (which import discards). An implementer could wrongly merge in a pre-existing target's workflow or apply defaults.
suggestion: State explicitly that `tp import` preserves the imported source file's workflow-field presence (no default materialization) and does not inherit any pre-existing target file's workflow block; if the source has no workflow key it normalizes to `{}` per §4.4.

finding: §3.2 makes an absolute closure claim: "any command that rewrites the file is bound by this invariant, and the list above is its current membership", under which every task-file-rewriting command leaves the set of present workflow fields unchanged (only tp set --workflow may change one field). But §7.1 affirms tp config --extract "still hoists shared policy into .tp/config.json and is unaffected"; hoisting shared workflow policy up to the project config removes those fields from the task files (thinning). That makes extract a file-rewriting command that intentionally DELETES present workflow fields, yet it is neither in §3.2's membership list nor the tp set --workflow exception — so §3.2's blanket claim is either incomplete or false, and an unaffected/still-active extract directly contradicts the round-trip invariant.
suggestion: Scope §3.2 strictly to the WriteTaskFile round-trip path and explicitly carve out tp config --extract as a second, intentional field-removing writer (as it already carves out tp set --workflow), or add extract to the membership with its documented thinning behavior. Reconcile the "any command that rewrites the file is bound" sentence with extract's hoist/thin semantics referenced in §7.1.

finding: `checks` is a `tp set --workflow`-settable workflow field in v0.24.0 (replace semantics), and its presence handling is a headline concern of this release (§4.3, §10.6). Yet §6.1 describes `tp set --workflow` validation only for the five integer fields ("validated against the field's range") and never covers setting `checks` via `tp set --workflow`: its value syntax on a `field=value` CLI, its validation, and whether the set creates field presence per §4.3 are all unspecified.
suggestion: In §6.1 state whether `checks` is settable via `tp set --workflow` in this release; if so, describe its value form, validation, and that the set marks the field present (§4.3, replace semantics); if not, say so and give the rejection exit code/hint.

finding: §6.1 specifies exit code 1 + hint only for a numeric value that is out of range (review_clean_rounds=0, audit_max_rounds=51). It says nothing about a value that does not parse as an integer at all (e.g. `tp set --workflow review_clean_rounds=abc`, `=1.5`, or empty) or an unknown/non-workflow field name (`tp set --workflow bogus=1`). The implementer must invent whether these are validation errors (exit 1) or usage errors (exit 2) and what hint is emitted.
suggestion: In §6.1 extend the error taxonomy: state the exit code (1 vs 2) and hint for (a) a value that fails to parse as the field's type, and (b) an unrecognized field name, and confirm the file is left unmodified in each case — matching the existing out-of-range clause.

finding: For an authored/imported task file carrying a workflow value outside its valid range (e.g. review_clean_rounds: 0, which §6.1 pins to 1-10), two normative rules collide. §3.5 says 'An invalid explicit value (e.g. review_clean_rounds: 0, outside 1-10) is a validation error surfaced at the point of write (§6.1)', but its '(§6.1)' cross-reference scopes validation to tp set --workflow; meanwhile §3.1/§7.1 say a present field in a pre-existing or imported task file 'is always treated as an override regardless of value' and 'stays in force until the file is deliberately thinned,' and §3.2 lists tp import among the WriteTaskFile commands. A tester cannot determine whether `tp import` (or first load) of an out-of-range workflow value must reject with exit 1 or accept-and-preserve it; two engineers write contradictory pass/fail tests.
suggestion: Pin whether the §6.1 range validation applies to the tp import / authored-file write path or only to tp set --workflow. Add one sentence to §7.1, e.g. 'tp import rejects an out-of-range workflow value with exit 1 (as in §6.1)' OR 'imported present fields are preserved verbatim without range re-validation; an out-of-range legacy value resolves as-is.' Then add a matching acceptance line under §10.

finding: §10 adds a dedicated round-trip acceptance test for `commit_strategy` (§10.7) and covers the five integers (§10.2) and `checks` (§10.6), but there is no round-trip test that a `{"quality_gate": "<cmd>"}` block survives the WriteTaskFile commands unchanged — even though `quality_gate` is the workflow field most likely to be non-empty in real task files (tp's own repo uses it) and is presence-tracked (§4.1).
suggestion: Add a §10 acceptance case: a task file with only `{"quality_gate": "<cmd>"}` keeps exactly that field across the §10.2 operations, and `tp config --resolved` attributes it to source "override" — parallel to the §10.7 commit_strategy test.

finding: Design Principle #3 says "only unset (default-materialized) fields stop being written." Read literally this suggests the write path should strip fields whose value equals the built-in default. But §3.5 says presence — not value — distinguishes an override, §7.1 says existing materialized files keep every present field until deliberately thinned, and §3.2 mandates round-trip preservation. So default-materialized fields do NOT "stop being written" for existing files; the principle is only true of tp's new sparse write path (it stops injecting defaults). The imprecise wording risks a value-based-stripping implementation that violates §3.2.
suggestion: Reword Principle #3 to describe write-path behavior only: tp stops MATERIALIZING (injecting) unset defaults into blocks it writes; existing present fields are always preserved (presence, not value, is authoritative — §3.5/§7.1).

finding: §4.1 promotes quality_gate and commit_strategy to first-class presence-tracked override fields (*string) inside the canonical WorkflowOverride, and §6.1 describes tp set --workflow <field>=<value> generically as "adds or updates exactly that field ... leaving every other field's presence untouched." Neither section reaffirms the existing invariant that quality_gate is read-only (authored only via tp init --quality-gate) and commit_strategy is written only by tp init --commit-strategy — i.e. neither is settable via tp set --workflow. Because these fields are now part of the all-pointer settable-looking struct, a setter built over it could silently expose them via tp set --workflow, breaking the read-only invariant this release does not intend to change.
suggestion: Add one sentence to §6.1 (or §4.1) reaffirming that tp set --workflow's settable field set is unchanged (the five convergence/timeout integers plus checks) and that quality_gate and commit_strategy remain init-authored only, even though they are now presence-tracked and round-tripped (§10.7).
